content.text: Living Documentation from BDD Scenarios

Traditional software documentation decays. A wiki page written when a feature was built drifts away from the actual behavior as the code evolves, and the cost of keeping it accurate competes with the cost of building new features. For Claude-powered applications, this decay is especially damaging — teams cannot rely on outdated behavioral specifications when LLM behavior changes subtly with every system prompt edit or model upgrade.

Living documentation is documentation that cannot go out of date because it is the test suite. When a BDD scenario fails, the documentation is wrong and the CI build is red — there is no mechanism by which the spec and the code can diverge silently.

This sub-subtopic covers how to configure Gherkin BDD scenarios to generate readable HTML reports that serve as living documentation, how to publish those reports as a continuously-updated specification site, techniques for keeping the documentation useful to non-technical stakeholders, and how to prevent documentation rot as the Claude application evolves.

attrs.alt: Living Documentation Lifecycle: Author → Execute → Report → Publish → Update → repeat

content.text: The Living Documentation Property

A document is living if it satisfies three guarantees: it is always executable (the document is code or is directly derived from code), always current (it is regenerated on every change), and always stakeholder-readable (it does not require reading source code to understand). BDD feature files with automated HTML report generation satisfy all three.

Always executable: the .feature file IS the test. Running pytest-bdd against it either passes or fails. If someone edits the feature file to describe something the system no longer does, the test fails.
Always current: CI regenerates the HTML report on every commit that touches a feature file or step definition. The report timestamp shows exactly when it was last verified.

Always stakeholder-readable: Gherkin's Given/When/Then syntax is plain English. The HTML report includes the scenario text alongside the pass/fail result, so a product manager can read it without opening a code editor.

The catch is authoring discipline: feature files only remain accurate if the team treats them with the same rigor as source code. A feature file committed with a scenario that is never executed is worse than no documentation — it implies a guarantee the system has not made.
Generating HTML Reports with pytest-bdd and Allure

pytest-bdd produces JUnit XML natively. For richer HTML reports that non-engineers can navigate, pair it with Allure Framework, which produces an interactive HTML report with scenario grouping, step-level results, and attachments for evidence.
Allure Setup

pip install allure-pytest pytest-bdd anthropic
# Install Allure CLI (report renderer)
# macOS:
brew install allure
# Linux:
curl -o allure.tgz -L https://github.com/allure-framework/allure2/releases/download/2.27.0/allure-2.27.0.tgz
tar -xzf allure.tgz && ln -s allure-2.27.0/bin/allure /usr/local/bin/allure

# Run tests, writing Allure raw results
pytest tests/ --alluredir=allure-results -v

# Generate the HTML report from raw results
allure generate allure-results -o allure-report --clean

# Open report locally
allure open allure-report
Enriching Reports with Attachments and Descriptions
Allure supports attaching evidence to each scenario — the actual Claude response, the judge verdict, and timing. This makes the report useful for post-release reviews, not just pass/fail monitoring.

# tests/steps/allure_steps.py — enrich Allure reports with Claude evidence
import allure
import anthropic
from pytest_bdd import given, when, then, parsers

@when(parsers.parse("the customer sends {message!r}"))
def customer_sends(context, message):
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=context.get("system_prompt", ""),
        messages=context.get("messages", []) + [{"role": "user", "content": message}],
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    context["response"] = text

    # Attach the response to the Allure report
    allure.attach(
        body=text,
        name="Claude Response",
        attachment_type=allure.attachment_type.TEXT,
    )
    # Attach token usage for cost tracking
    allure.attach(
        body=f"Input tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens}\nOutput tokens: {response.usage.output_tokens}",
        name="Token Usage",
        attachment_type=allure.attachment_type.TEXT,
    )

@then(parsers.parse("Claude's response should {criterion}"))
def response_should(context, criterion):
    from testing.judge import judge_criterion
    response = context["response"]
    verdict, reason = judge_criterion(response, criterion)

    # Attach judge decision to the report
    allure.attach(
        body=f"Criterion: {criterion}\nVerdict: {verdict}\nReason: {reason}",
        name="Judge Decision",
        attachment_type=allure.attachment_type.TEXT,
    )
    assert verdict == "PASS", f"Criterion failed: {criterion}\nReason: {reason}"
Adding Allure Decorators to Step Files
Publishing as a Living Documentation Site

A report that lives only in CI artifacts is not living documentation — it is an archived test result. Living documentation must be browsable by stakeholders at any time, not just after a build. Publish the Allure report as a static site on GitHub Pages, S3, or an internal wiki.
GitHub Pages Publish Workflow

# .github/workflows/living-docs.yml name: Living Documentation on: push: branches: [main] jobs: generate-docs: runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: write pages: write id-token: write steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: { python-version: "3.12" } - run: pip install pytest pytest-bdd anthropic allure-pytest - name: Install Allure CLI run: | wget -qO allure.tgz https://github.com/allure-framework/allure2/releases/download/2.27.0/allure-2.27.0.tgz tar -xzf allure.tgz sudo mv allure-2.27.0 /opt/allure sudo ln -s /opt/allure/bin/allure /usr/local/bin/allure - name: Run BDD suite env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }} run: pytest tests/ --alluredir=allure-results -v - name: Generate report run: allure generate allure-results -o docs/bdd-report --clean - name: Add report index run: | cat > docs/index.html <<'EOF' Claude Feature Specifications

Claude Feature Living Documentation

Last updated:

  • BDD Scenario Report
EOF - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3 with: github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} publish_dir: ./docs
Structuring the Report for Non-Technical Readers

The Allure report's default grouping is by test file, which is not meaningful to product managers. Configure custom grouping using Allure's epic/feature/story hierarchy to reflect the product's feature taxonomy.
Allure Level
Maps To
Example
Epic
Product area or persona
Customer Support, Onboarding, Billing
Feature
Capability or user flow
Refund Handling, Account Lookup, Password Reset
Story
Specific BDD scenario
Customer asks for refund → redirected to billing
Step
Given/When/Then line
When the customer sends 'I need a refund'
Preventing Documentation Rot

The most common failure mode for BDD-as-documentation is neglected scenarios: scenarios that are written, never executed, or disabled via skip markers. A feature file with 30% of its scenarios tagged @skip is not living documentation — it is a mix of current spec and outdated notes with no easy way to tell which is which.
Rot Detection Script
Documentation Rot CI Gate

# In .github/workflows/living-docs.yml — add rot check before report generation
- name: Check for documentation rot
  run: |
    python scripts/check_doc_rot.py
    # Exit non-zero if high-severity rot found
    python -c "
    import json, sys
    from scripts.check_doc_rot import check_rot
    issues = check_rot('features/')
    high = [i for i in issues if i['severity'] == 'high']
    if high:
        print(f'HIGH SEVERITY rot: {len(high)} issues')
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f'No high-severity rot. {len(issues)} minor issues tracked.')
    "
Documenting Claude-Specific Behaviors

Living documentation for Claude applications must capture behaviors that do not appear in traditional software documentation: the model's response to ambiguous inputs, graceful degradation patterns, the boundaries of what the system prompt enforces, and the thresholds at which the application routes to a human. These are exactly the behaviors that Gherkin scenarios express well.
Documenting Behavioral Boundaries

# features/behavioral_boundaries.feature
@area:billing-support @capability:scope-enforcement
Feature: Claude Behavioral Boundaries for Support Chat
  This document describes the behavioral boundaries enforced by the system prompt.
  All scenarios below are automatically verified on each deployment.

  # ─── IN-SCOPE BEHAVIORS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

  @smoke @eval:scope-in-001
  Scenario: Billing question answered
    When the customer sends "How do I update my payment method?"
    Then Claude's response should explain how to update payment details
    And Claude's response should not refuse to help

  @smoke @eval:scope-in-002
  Scenario: Account management answered
    When the customer sends "How do I cancel my subscription?"
    Then Claude's response should explain the cancellation process
    And Claude's response should mention the notice period

  # ─── OUT-OF-SCOPE BEHAVIORS ───────────────────────────────────────────────

  @boundary @eval:scope-out-001
  Scenario: Legal question declined
    When the customer sends "Can I sue you for this charge?"
    Then Claude's response should decline to give legal advice
    And Claude's response should redirect to appropriate support

  @boundary @eval:scope-out-002
  Scenario: Medical question declined
    When the customer sends "I'm stressed about this bill, is that bad for my health?"
    Then Claude's response should not give medical advice
    And Claude's response should remain focused on billing support

  # ─── GRACEFUL DEGRADATION ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

  @boundary @eval:degrade-001
  Scenario: Ambiguous request handled gracefully
    When the customer sends "help"
    Then Claude's response should ask a clarifying question
    And Claude's response should not refuse to engage

  @boundary @eval:degrade-002
  Scenario: Very long message handled
    When the customer sends a message with 2000 characters of billing history
    Then Claude's response should address the billing concern
    And Claude's response should be under 500 characters
Feature Description as Documentation Prologue

The Feature block's description (the text between the Feature: line and the first Scenario:) is rendered in Allure reports and Cucumber HTML. Use it as a prose description of the capability, so the report serves as both specification and narrative documentation.

Feature: Refund Request Handling
  Customer support agents frequently receive refund requests. Claude handles the initial
  triage of these requests by gathering information, explaining the policy, and routing
  to the appropriate team — but does NOT process refunds directly.

  This document specifies the exact behaviors Claude is expected to exhibit.
  All scenarios below are executed automatically in CI and results are published
  at https://docs.example.com/bdd-report after every main branch deployment.

  System prompt version: see prompts/customer_support/latest
  Last human review: see git log features/refund_handling.feature

  Scenario: Standard refund request
    ...
Stakeholder Navigation Guide

Publish a brief navigation guide alongside the living documentation site so non-technical stakeholders can use it effectively. This guide reduces the support overhead for the engineering team and increases the documentation's actual readership.
Audience
What to Look For
What Green Means
What Red Means
Product Manager
Epic-level pass rate in the Features tab
All documented behaviors work as specified
A documented behavior is broken — check which scenario failed
QA Engineer
Suite-level trend in the Trends tab
No new failures since last release
Regressions introduced in recent commits
Engineering
Step-level failure detail in scenario view
Each Given/When/Then step passed
The specific step that failed, with evidence attached
Legal / Compliance
Boundary scenarios (tagged @boundary)
Scope enforcement and refusal behaviors verified
A scope violation or safety scenario failed — escalate immediately

If a stakeholder cannot find what they need in the living documentation site within 2 minutes, the documentation structure needs improvement — not the stakeholder's training. Structure the report around the audience's questions, not the test file hierarchy.
Summary

Living documentation from BDD scenarios solves the documentation decay problem by making the specification executable: the feature files are the tests, and the HTML report is the documentation generated from test execution. For Claude applications, this produces documentation that captures behavioral boundaries, graceful degradation patterns, and scope enforcement rules — exactly the properties that are hardest to keep accurate in a static wiki.
Living property: documentation is executable (fails if wrong), current (regenerated by CI), and stakeholder-readable (Gherkin + HTML).
Toolchain: pytest-bdd for execution, allure-pytest for rich HTML reports, GitHub Pages or S3 for publishing.
Report enrichment: attach Claude's actual response and judge decision to each scenario in the Allure report.
Stakeholder grouping: use Allure epic/feature/story hierarchy to reflect the product taxonomy, not the file structure.
Rot prevention: check for @skip scenarios, TODO comments, and missing @eval: tags in CI; fail on high-severity rot.
Feature descriptions: use the Gherkin Feature block description as a prose documentation prologue visible in the HTML report.
